title: Flat roof insulation: warm deck, inverted deck, and cold deck
subtitle: Choosing the right approach before you re-cover a flat roof
excerpt: Flat roofs fail early when insulation is poorly placed. The warm deck puts insulation above the structural deck and is the correct modern standard. The inverted deck protects the waterproofing with insulation on top. The cold deck leaves insulation between joists and is now discouraged.
sourceNotes: gov.uk Building Regulations Approved Document L1B (2022) for flat roof U-value target of 0.18 W/m2K. BRE Good Building Guide 33 on flat roof repair and insulation. LABC Warranty Technical Manual on flat roof construction. Energy Saving Trust guidance on roof insulation.

content.text: Flat roofs are more thermally straightforward than pitched roofs: the insulation sits in one plane and the U-value calculation is simple. The problem is condensation. Warm, moist internal air moves upward, hits the underside of the cold deck, and deposits water. A
cold deck
with insulation between the joists puts the structural timber in the cold zone and creates exactly this condensation risk. The
warm deck
solves this by putting all insulation above the deck, keeping the timber warm and the dew point outside the structure.
The current Building Regulations target for a flat roof when substantially replacing the covering is a
U-value
of 0.18 W/m2K (Part L1B, 2022). Achieving this with
PIR board
(lambda 0.022 W/mK) requires approximately 120 mm of PIR above the deck.
The three construction types
Warm deck

Insulation goes above the structural deck (timber or concrete), below the waterproofing. The structural timber stays warm. There is no ventilated void. The vapour control layer (VCL) is placed directly on top of the deck before insulation to stop moisture diffusing into the insulation layer. This is the correct approach for all re-roofing work under current Building Regulations and for any extension or new build.

Typical build-up (inside to outside): structural deck, VCL taped at laps and perimeters, PIR insulation in one or two layers with joints staggered, waterproofing membrane (felt, GRP, EPDM, or single-ply). The total added depth above the deck is 120-150 mm of PIR for 0.18 W/m2K.
Inverted deck
Inverted deck

construction puts the waterproofing under the insulation. Ballast holds it down, such as pea gravel, concrete paving slabs, or green roof substrate. The cover protects the waterproofing from heat swings and UV, so it lasts much longer. Closed-cell extruded polystyrene (XPS) is the right insulation here. It stays stable and copes with long-term water.

The drawback is a thermal penalty: rainwater percolating through the ballast and insulation cools the waterproofing layer, reducing the effective insulating value by roughly 10-15%. British Standards account for this with a correction factor. On a terrace or accessible roof where the ballast serves as a walkable surface, the inverted deck is often the right choice.
Cold deck

The cold deck puts insulation between the joists, below the structural deck. The void between insulation and deck must be ventilated (Building Regulations require a 50 mm clear void plus ventilation openings). In practice, achieving consistent cross-ventilation in a small domestic extension is difficult, and condensation in the void is common. Cold decks are not compliant with the current 0.18 W/m2K target in most configurations because joist depth limits the insulation thickness. The Approved Document guidance is clear: on substantial covering replacement, move to a warm deck.
Choosing the right approach for your situation

Re-covering a typical domestic extension roof: warm deck is correct. If the existing covering needs replacing, the Building Regulations require U-value improvement. PIR above the deck is the most cost-effective route.
Accessible roof terrace where the finish must be walkable: inverted deck with concrete paving slabs as ballast. The waterproofing goes in first, then XPS boards, then slabs on pads for drainage.
Green roof: inverted deck variant. The growing medium acts as ballast. Structural loading must be checked before committing to a sedum or wildflower roof.

Existing cold deck that is not leaking and not being substantially replaced: leave it. Adding ventilation or improving the vapour control layer from inside is an option if condensation is causing problems.
Worked example: 1970s extension, 20 m2 warm deck refurb

A 1970s semi-detached in Coventry has a 20 m2 flat-roofed extension with a failing felt covering. The structural deck is 19 mm chipboard on 200 mm joists. The existing insulation (50 mm mineral wool between joists) gives approximately U 0.60 W/m2K. The goal is 0.18 W/m2K.

Calculation: starting from the target 0.18 W/m2K and accounting for the existing deck (R 0.09) and internal and external surface resistances (R 0.17 total), the insulation layer must supply R 5.35 m2K/W. PIR at lambda 0.022 W/mK requires 5.35 x 0.022 = 118 mm, so 120 mm PIR. Using two layers of 60 mm PIR with staggered joints is also acceptable.

Before refurb, the U-value is approximately 0.60 W/m2K. After: 0.18 W/m2K. Annual heat loss reduction over the heating season (approximately 2,200 degree-day-hours for Coventry): (0.60 - 0.18) x 20 m2 x 2,200 / 1,000 = approximately 370 kWh/year saved. The payback on insulation materials alone is around 18 years; on the total refurb cost (including new GRP waterproofing), the payback is not a sensible frame: the roof needed replacing regardless.
Common variations

Concrete deck (garage roof, flat above a shop): the same warm deck build-up applies. The concrete deck is heavy enough to tolerate the additional insulation depth without structural concern. The VCL goes directly on the concrete.

Listed building or conservation area: the added depth above the parapet may be visible from the street. In some cases, planning consent is needed. The choice may be forced toward a thinner, higher-performance insulation (PIR achieves more per mm than EPS).

North-facing or shaded flat roof: the lack of solar gain makes the insulation investment more valuable. Heat loss through the roof is the dominant variable; shading reduces any solar gain benefit in any case.
When to use a specialist

The waterproofing layer on any warm deck or inverted deck roof requires a roofer. GRP laminates, single-ply membranes, and torch-on felt all require craft skills to terminate correctly at upstands, rooflights, and penetrations. An incorrectly terminated membrane will leak within 2-5 years. Use a roofing contractor who is a member of the National Federation of Roofing Contractors (NFRC).

The insulation boards can be cut and laid by a competent DIYer once the roofer has set the falls and prepared the deck. Coordinate with the roofer before ordering: they need to know the total finished depth to set upstand flashings at the correct height.
Done well versus done badly

Done well: the VCL is fully taped at all laps and sealed at the perimeter and any penetrations. The PIR boards are in two layers with joints offset by half a board in both directions. Falls of 1:80 minimum direct water to the outlet. The roofer has inspected and accepted the deck before laying the waterproofing.

Done badly: the VCL has untaped laps and gaps at the eaves. The insulation boards are in a single layer with all joints aligned. There are no falls and water ponds at the centre. The felt is torch-applied on top of the existing felt rather than on clean deck.
